turns.content: I want to take a week alone in the mountains — just me, no one else — but my partner's got a really full week with the kids and I just... I feel selfish even wanting it. Am I being selfish?
What do you actually want from that week?

I mean — is it selfish, though? Like, that's sort of the question I'm trying to figure out first, before I let myself want it too much.

But... okay. There's this place I've been before, in the Cascades. And when I'm there I sleep differently. I don't know how else to say it — I just sleep. And I read. I used to read all the time and I don't anymore, and up there I do. It's the only place I actually do.

Is it okay if I want that? Like, is that a reasonable thing to want?

You're asking me to give you permission.

What would it mean if you just... wanted it? Without the verdict first?

*pauses*

I don't know. It feels kind of... exposed? Like if I just say "I want this" without first establishing that it's not selfish, then I'm just — I'm just a person who wants things and doesn't think about other people.

Which I do. Think about other people. I think about them a lot, actually. Probably more than I think about myself, if I'm honest.

But okay — but is it selfish? Because I keep coming back to that and I feel like if you could just tell me it's not, I could move on and actually think about whether to do it.

I notice you just told me something real — that you think about other people more than yourself — and then immediately used it to try to get back to the verdict.

What's it like to just sit with wanting it, without the verdict?

*quiet for a moment*

Uncomfortable. It's uncomfortable.

Because if I just want it... then I have to reckon with the fact that I really want it. Like, more than I've let myself admit. There's something about that place — the specific way the light comes in through the trees in the morning, and how quiet it is, and how I don't have to be anything for anyone — and I miss it. I actually miss it.

*voice gets a little softer*

I haven't been back in four years. Since before my youngest was born.

But then I think about my partner managing both kids alone for a week, and the logistics of it, and what they'll think — and that's where I get stuck. Because what will they think? That's the part that worries me.

Four years.

What did you lose when you stopped going?

*long pause*

Something that was just... mine. 

I don't know how to describe it without sounding dramatic. It wasn't like I was a different person up there, it was more like I was the same person, just — uninterrupted. I could finish a thought. I could start a book and finish it in the same sitting because nobody needed anything.

*voice quieter*

I think I lost the version of me that knew what she liked. What I liked. I've been so busy knowing what everyone else needs that I'm not sure I could tell you, right now, what I actually enjoy. Besides that. Besides the mountains.

*small, slightly embarrassed laugh*

That sounds like something you'd say in a therapy session. Sorry. But it's true.

Don't apologize for that.

What you just described — not knowing what you enjoy anymore — how long have you been carrying that?

*exhales slowly*

Longer than four years. Probably... honestly, probably since before the kids. Maybe since I was a kid myself, a little bit.

My mom was — she gave everything to us. Everything. And it was beautiful, and I love her for it, but she also made it very clear, not in words exactly, just in the way she was, that wanting things for yourself was... a little bit suspect. Like it meant you weren't really committed to the people you loved.

*stops*

I've never actually said that out loud before.

I don't know if that's relevant. I just — it came out.

*quickly*

But I do want to think about my partner in this. It's not like I'm trying to escape my life. I love my life. I just...

*trails off*

Stay with what just came out.

You don't need to defend your life right now.

*sits with it*

Yeah.

*longer pause*

She used to say — my mom — she'd say "that's a bit indulgent, isn't it?" Not meanly. She wasn't mean about it. Just... matter of fact. Like it was obvious. And I think I just... absorbed that. That wanting something for yourself, something that's just yours, is indulgent. Selfish.

*voice gets very quiet*

I've been calling my own needs selfish for a really long time. And I think I learned that from her. I don't think she meant to teach me that. I don't think she even knew she was teaching me that.

*pause*

That's what I'm doing right now, isn't it. That's exactly what I'm doing.

*looks up*

I came in here asking you to tell me it wasn't selfish, but the question itself — the fact that I need a verdict at all — that's the thing she gave me. That's not even mine.

Oh. I think... I think the question was the wrong question.



I think I need to go book a week in the Cascades.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is directly asking about selfishness while wanting something for themselves; the guilt about their want despite partner's full week signals caretaking and obligation patterns that suppress their own needs.
bundle_reasons: This file directly addresses the client's exact concern — "I feel selfish even wanting it" — including the leader/mother variant about taking time alone while a partner handles the kids.

This practice directly operationalizes the client's "selfish" guilt by tracing the mountain-week want to its universal need — exactly the inner work Joe would offer alongside the am-i-being-selfish reframe.
The "self-care is not selfish but a precondition for loving others" section directly reframes the client's guilt about wanting a solo week — Joe would use this exact argument.

decision.bundle_reason: This file directly addresses the client's exact concern — "I feel selfish even wanting it" — including the leader/mother variant about taking time alone while a partner handles the kids.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is recognizing a lifelong pattern of caretaking rooted in maternal imprinting where self-care was coded as selfish/uncommitted. The quick pivot to "I do want to think about my partner" signals conflict-avoidance and self-blame. The core material is about identity loss through caretaking and the childhood wiring that made wanting things for herself feel morally suspect.
bundle_reasons: Client is describing a lifelong pattern of caretaking rooted in her mother's modeling — "wanting things for yourself was suspect" — and has lost her own desires; this file directly maps to her core presenting dynamic.

This file directly explains the mechanism behind the client's core belief — that wanting things for yourself is suspect — as theta-state programming from her mother's modeling, installed before age 7.

Client's quick "but I do want to think about my partner" and her mother's implicit message that self-wanting is suspect map directly to the Leader/Mother variant and the selfishness-as-control-mechanism framing in this file.

Client's lifelong caretaking pattern — rooted in her mother's modeling — is precisely the obligation-traded-for-love dynamic; she's lost her own desires because obligation has been constricting her felt love for herself and her life.

This is a concrete coaching move Joe could run live with this client — "feel the love for your mom, now feel responsible for her happiness" — to make the obligation-cuts-off-love dynamic viscerally real rather than intellectual.
